skill.name: uspto-database
skill.description: Access USPTO patent and trademark APIs for searches, prosecution history, assignments, citations, office actions, and portfolio research.
skill.summary: Research patents, trademarks, and USPTO prosecution data from official public APIs.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Manual review found that the critical and high static findings are mostly false positives from Markdown examples, USPTO terminology, and placeholder API-key text. The confirmed risk is legitimate outbound access to official USPTO APIs using a user-provided API key, plus one CLI option that can expose an API key through shell history. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, arbitrary command execution, or obfuscation.

medium_findings.title: Outbound USPTO API Requests Use User API Key
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts read USPTO_API_KEY or accept an API key and send it as X-Api-Key to USPTO API endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand that queries and the key are sent to external USPTO services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct code shows API-key loading and requests to named USPTO endpoints. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is disclosure and handling rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Command-Line API Key Exposure Option
medium_findings.description: The trademark CLI accepts --api-key, which may expose a USPTO API key in shell history or process listings. Environment variables are documented elsewhere and are safer for routine use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The argparse option is directly present and passes the key into the client. Exposure depends on how users run the command, so the risk is practical but not proof of malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline examples in documentation. Manual review did not find subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec usage in the executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation blocks or setup examples, not executable Ruby or shell backtick calls. A targeted search found no executable command-launch primitives in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Findings Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM finding maps to the USPTO conveyance term SECURITY AGREEMENT, and weak-crypto hits map to product names or text fragments. No evidence found of credential database access or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited sensitive term is business terminology for assignments. The reviewed files contain API documentation and client code, not credential store paths or crypto routines.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined network access, API-key references, and documentation examples into a critical heuristic. Manual review found official USPTO destinations and no evidence of hidden third-party exfiltration or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous components exist only in a transparent USPTO API-client context. Suspicious instruction phrases and command-execution primitives were not found during targeted review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Requests Carry API Key Headers
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts make outbound HTTPS requests with X-Api-Key headers. This is legitimate for USPTO access but should remain visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs X-Api-Key headers and sends requests. The endpoint context lowers maliciousness but confirms the pattern.

content.user_title: Research USPTO Patent and Trademark Data
content.value_statement: Patent and trademark research often requires several USPTO systems and changing API formats. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance and helper scripts for official USPTO data workflows.
actual_capabilities: Search patents by keyword, inventor, assignee, date range, and CPC classification.
Retrieve patent details such as abstracts, titles, inventors, assignees, classifications, and citations.
Query trademark records by serial or registration number and summarize status information.
Access PEDS workflows for application status, transaction history, office actions, and prosecution analysis.
Reference USPTO assignment, citation, office action, PTAB, litigation, and related data APIs.
Use included Python helper scripts for common patent, trademark, and PEDS queries.
limitations: Most USPTO API calls require a user-provided USPTO API key.
Results depend on USPTO API availability, rate limits, and data coverage.
The PEDS helper is a simplified wrapper around uspto-opendata-python.
The skill supports research workflows but does not provide legal advice.

use_cases.title: Prior Art Search Preparation
use_cases.description: Find patents by technical terms, inventors, assignees, and classifications before a deeper review.

use_cases.title: Prosecution History Review
use_cases.description: Summarize application status, office actions, transaction history, and response patterns from USPTO records.

use_cases.title: Trademark Portfolio Monitoring
use_cases.description: Check trademark status, owner details, goods and services, and prosecution history for tracked marks.

prompt_templates.title: Find Related Patents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the USPTO database skill to search for patents about [technology topic]. Focus on recent grants and summarize the strongest matches.

prompt_templates.title: Check Trademark Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Look up trademark serial number [serial number]. Summarize current status, filing date, owner information, and any prosecution events.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Patent Prosecution
prompt_templates.prompt: Review application number [application number] using PEDS. Identify office actions, applicant responses, current status, and notable timing.

prompt_templates.title: Build Portfolio Research Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a USPTO research workflow for [company name]. Include patent search, assignments, citations, PTAB checks, litigation checks, and trademark status review.

output_examples.input: Search for machine learning patents assigned to a named company.
output_examples.output: A ranked patent list with titles, patent numbers, issue dates, assignees, abstract notes, and recommended records for full review.

output_examples.input: Review a trademark serial number for status and ownership.
output_examples.output: A concise trademark status brief with mark text, status date, registration details, owner information, and prosecution highlights.

output_examples.input: Analyze prosecution history for one patent application.
output_examples.output: A timeline summary covering filing date, office actions, responses, allowance or abandonment indicators, and open follow-up questions.

best_practices: Store the USPTO API key in USPTO_API_KEY instead of passing it on the command line.
Start with narrow search fields, then broaden terms after reviewing early results.
Record query terms, filters, dates, and API sources for repeatable research.
anti_patterns: Do not treat API search results as a final patentability or clearance opinion.
Do not publish or share API keys in prompts, logs, tickets, or examples.
Do not ignore USPTO rate limits or retry errors without backoff.

faq.question: Does this skill require a USPTO API key?
faq.answer: Yes. Most workflows require a USPTO API key stored as USPTO_API_KEY or passed to a client.

faq.question: Can it search both patents and trademarks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance and scripts for PatentSearch, PEDS, and TSDR trademark workflows.

faq.question: Does it provide legal advice?
faq.answer: No. It helps collect and organize public USPTO data for review by qualified professionals.

faq.question: Can it retrieve prosecution history?
faq.answer: Yes. The PEDS workflow can summarize status, transaction history, office actions, and prosecution events.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What are the main security considerations?
faq.answer: The skill sends queries to USPTO APIs and uses an API key. Keep the key out of prompts, logs, and command history.
